title: My mentor is sixty years older than me
summary: Reflect on a working friendship between a young apprentice and a retiree decades older, and what each unexpectedly teaches the other across a sixty-year gap.
topic: What can two people separated by sixty years genuinely teach each other?
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) reflecting on a friendship across a large age gap — a young person and someone roughly sixty years older — and what each one learns from the other that people their own age could not teach them. Avoid clichés about 'wisdom of the elderly'; be specific and two-directional. Use present perfect at least once, a conditional at least once, and 'although' or 'however' at least once.
hintStarters: When I started the job, I assumed the only thing an eighty-year-old and I could share was…
Although we disagree about almost everything from music to politics, the one place we meet completely is…
If I had only ever spent time with people my own age, I think I would still believe that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một người cố vấn, người dìu dắt

hintVocab.phrase: to keep up with the times

hintVocab.meaningVi: bắc cầu qua một sự chia cách

hintVocab.phrase: to treat someone as an equal
hintVocab.meaningVi: đối xử với ai đó như người ngang hàng
nhìn thế giới một cách khác
trưởng thành cùng với ai đó

sampleText:
When I started repairing watches at nineteen, I was paired with Bernard, who was seventy-nine and had been doing the same work since before my parents were born. On the first day I assumed the arrangement would be one-directional: he would teach, I would listen, and the only thing we would share was a workbench. We could not have been more different. He distrusts everything with a screen; I had never owned a mechanical watch I could not control from my phone. If you had asked me then, I would have predicted a polite, distant relationship that ended the moment I learned enough to work alone.

What actually happened surprised us both. Bernard taught me the obvious things — how to free a seized spring, how to listen to a movement rather than just look at it — but the real lesson was about time itself. He works on a scale I had never met in anyone my own age. He will spend an afternoon on a repair that earns almost nothing, because, as he puts it, the watch will outlast the customer and probably him, and someone in fifty years will open it and see whether the work was honest. I had never once thought about a stranger fifty years from now. Although I came to learn a trade, I have ended up learning a way of measuring my own actions that none of my friends could have shown me, simply because none of them had lived long enough to mean it.

The traffic, however, runs both ways, and this is the part people forget when they talk about old age and wisdom. Bernard has learned things from me too. I showed him how to find spare parts online that he had spent years writing letters to obtain, and more than that, I think I reminded him that his knowledge was still wanted — that a nineteen-year-old would choose to sit beside him and ask him questions. If we had each stayed among people our own age, he would have drifted into being someone whose skills the world had quietly stopped needing, and I would have stayed someone who measured everything by the next month. Neither of us would have been wrong, exactly; we would just have been smaller. The gap between us turned out not to be a barrier but the whole point. You cannot get a sixty-year head start on your own life. You can only borrow one, from someone generous enough to lend it.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for an outcome that still defines the present
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have ended up learning a way of measuring my own actions' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành 'have ended up' để diễn tả một kết quả bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn định hình con người hiện tại của người kể. 'End up + V-ing' nghĩa là 'rốt cuộc lại làm gì đó'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have ended up learning a way of measuring my own actions' uses the present perfect 'have ended up' to express a result that began in the past and still defines who the speaker is now. 'End up + -ing' means 'to finally find oneself doing something'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional to imagine a poorer alternative life
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If we had each stayed among people our own age, he would have drifted… and I would have stayed…' dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 (if + past perfect, would have + V3) để tưởng tượng một cuộc sống KHÔNG xảy ra. Nó cho thấy giá trị của tình bạn thực tế bằng cách phác họa điều gì sẽ mất nếu không có nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If we had each stayed among people our own age, he would have drifted… and I would have stayed…' uses the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + past participle) to imagine a life that did NOT happen. It shows the value of the real friendship by sketching what would have been lost without it.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi tôi bắt đầu sửa đồng hồ ở tuổi mười chín, tôi được ghép cặp với Bernard, người đã bảy mươi chín tuổi và đã làm cùng công việc này từ trước khi bố mẹ tôi ra đời. Ngày đầu tiên tôi cho rằng sự sắp xếp này sẽ chỉ đi theo một chiều: ông sẽ dạy, tôi sẽ nghe, và điều duy nhất chúng tôi chia sẻ là một chiếc bàn làm việc. Chúng tôi không thể nào khác nhau hơn được nữa. Ông ngờ vực mọi thứ có màn hình; tôi thì chưa từng sở hữu một chiếc đồng hồ cơ nào mà tôi không thể điều khiển từ điện thoại của mình. Nếu bạn hỏi tôi lúc đó, tôi sẽ dự đoán một mối quan hệ lịch sự, xa cách, kết thúc ngay khoảnh khắc tôi học đủ để làm việc một mình.

Điều thực sự xảy ra làm cả hai chúng tôi ngạc nhiên. Bernard dạy tôi những điều hiển nhiên — cách gỡ một chiếc lò xo bị kẹt, cách lắng nghe một bộ máy thay vì chỉ nhìn vào nó — nhưng bài học thực sự là về chính thời gian. Ông làm việc ở một quy mô mà tôi chưa từng gặp ở bất kỳ ai cùng tuổi mình. Ông sẽ dành cả một buổi chiều cho một ca sửa kiếm được gần như chẳng bao nhiêu, bởi vì, như ông nói, chiếc đồng hồ sẽ sống lâu hơn người khách và có lẽ lâu hơn cả ông, và ai đó năm mươi năm sau sẽ mở nó ra và xem liệu công việc có trung thực hay không. Tôi chưa một lần nghĩ về một người lạ ở năm mươi năm sau. Mặc dù tôi đến để học một cái nghề, tôi lại rốt cuộc học được một cách đo lường hành động của chính mình mà không người bạn nào của tôi có thể chỉ cho tôi, đơn giản vì không ai trong số họ đã sống đủ lâu để nói điều đó một cách thật lòng.

Tuy nhiên, dòng chảy đi theo cả hai chiều, và đây là phần mà người ta quên mất khi họ nói về tuổi già và sự khôn ngoan. Bernard cũng đã học được nhiều điều từ tôi. Tôi chỉ cho ông cách tìm linh kiện thay thế trên mạng mà ông đã mất nhiều năm viết thư để có được, và hơn thế nữa, tôi nghĩ tôi nhắc ông rằng kiến thức của ông vẫn còn được cần đến — rằng một cậu thanh niên mười chín tuổi lại chọn ngồi bên cạnh ông và hỏi ông những câu hỏi. Nếu mỗi chúng tôi cứ ở mãi giữa những người cùng tuổi mình, ông sẽ trôi dần vào việc trở thành một người mà thế giới đã lặng lẽ thôi cần đến kỹ năng của họ, và tôi sẽ vẫn là một người đo lường mọi thứ bằng tháng tới. Không ai trong chúng tôi sẽ sai, một cách chính xác; chúng tôi chỉ là sẽ trở nên nhỏ bé hơn. Khoảng cách giữa chúng tôi hóa ra không phải một rào cản mà chính là điều cốt lõi. Bạn không thể có một khởi đầu trước sáu mươi năm trong chính cuộc đời mình. Bạn chỉ có thể mượn nó, từ một người đủ hào phóng để cho mượn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect with 'end up' for a lasting outcome?
options: I have ended up to learn a new way of measuring my actions.
I have ended up learning a new way of measuring my actions.
I have ended up learn a new way of measuring my actions.
I have end up learning a new way of measuring my actions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have ended up + gerund' is correct. Option 2 uses the infinitive 'to learn'. Option 3 uses the base verb 'learn'. Option 4 uses 'end up' instead of the participle 'ended up' after 'have'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'End up' is followed by '-ing', and present perfect needs the participle 'ended'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly to imagine a different life?
options: If we had stayed among our own age groups, we would been smaller people.
If we would have stayed among our own age groups, we would have been smaller people.
If we had stayed among our own age groups, we would have been smaller people.
If we stayed among our own age groups, we would have been smaller people.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional = if + past perfect, would have + past participle. Option 2 drops 'have'. Option 3 puts 'would have' in the if-clause. Option 4 uses past simple in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Unreal past: if + had + participle, then would have + participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede the speaker's original goal?
options: Although I came to learn a trade, but I learned a way of measuring my actions.
Although I came to learn a trade I learned a way of measuring my actions.
I came to learn a trade, although however I learned a way of measuring my actions.
Although I came to learn a trade, I learned a way of measuring my actions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, + main clause' is correct. Option 2 adds 'but'. Option 3 omits the comma. Option 4 stacks 'although' and 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' needs a comma and stands alone, without 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes a friendship few would expect?
options: It became an unlikely friendship.
It became an unliked friendship.
It became a not likely friendship.
It became an unlikable friendship.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'An unlikely friendship' (one you would not expect) is the natural collocation. 'Unliked' means not liked. 'A not likely friendship' is clumsy and ungrammatical as a phrase. 'Unlikable' means hard to like.
mcQuestions.hint: Which adjective means 'not expected' rather than 'not liked'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'could not have been more + adjective'?
options: We could not been more different.
We could not have been more different.
We could not have be more different.
We could not have been more differently.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could not have been more + adjective' is the correct emphatic structure. Option 2 drops 'have'. Option 3 uses 'be' instead of 'been'. Option 4 uses the adverb 'differently' where an adjective is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'could not have been more' plus an adjective.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a third conditional in a question about the past?
options: If you had asked me then, I would predicted a distant relationship.
If you asked me then, I would have predicted a distant relationship.
If you had asked me then, I would have predicted a distant relationship.
If you had asked me then, I will have predicted a distant relationship.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional = if + past perfect, would have + past participle. Option 2 drops 'have'. Option 3 uses past simple in the if-clause. Option 4 uses 'will have', which cannot describe an unreal past.
mcQuestions.hint: Unreal past: if + had + participle, then would have + participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes contributions going in both directions?
options: The learning runs the both ways.
The learning runs in both way.
The learning runs to both ways.
The learning runs both ways.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Run both ways' is the natural idiom for something mutual. Option 2 adds 'the'. Option 3 uses singular 'way'. Option 4 adds an unnecessary 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: The idiom is 'both ways', with no article and the plural 'ways'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'long enough to + verb'?
options: None of them had lived long enough to mean it.
None of them had lived long enough for mean it.
None of them had lived enough long to mean it.
None of them had lived long enough meaning it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Long enough to + base verb' is correct. Option 2 uses 'for mean'. Option 3 reverses to 'enough long'. Option 4 uses '-ing'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'long enough to + verb'; 'enough' follows the adjective.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'remind someone that + clause'?
options: I reminded him that his knowledge was still want.
I reminded him that his knowledge was still wanted.
I reminded to him that his knowledge was still wanted.
I reminded him his knowledge being still wanted.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Remind someone that + clause' is correct, with the participle 'wanted' (still needed). Option 2 uses 'want' instead of 'wanted'. Option 3 adds 'to' after 'reminded'. Option 4 replaces the clause with an awkward '-ing' phrase.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Remind someone that' takes no 'to'; the watch knowledge is still 'wanted'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best states the article's closing image clearly and naturally?
options: You cannot get a sixty-year head start on your own life, you can only borrow one.
You cannot get a sixty-year head start on your own life; you can only to borrow one.
You cannot get a sixty-year head start on your own life; you can only borrow one.
You cannot get a sixty-years head start on your own life; you can only borrow one.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option 1 uses a semicolon and the bare infinitive 'borrow' after 'can'. Option 2 is a comma splice. Option 3 says 'to borrow' after 'can'. Option 4 uses 'sixty-years' where the compound adjective should be 'sixty-year'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use a semicolon, the bare infinitive after 'can', and the singular form in 'sixty-year head start'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer insists the learning between him and Bernard 'runs both ways', rejecting the usual one-way story of the old teaching the young. Have you ever taught something important to someone much older, or learned something from someone much younger? Why do you think society so often imagines this exchange as going in only one direction?
